attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a two-week history of daily fevers, joint pain, and a rash. The fevers spike to 39°C each evening and are associated with severe fatigue. The rash appears during fever episodes as salmon-pink patches on her trunk and proximal limbs, fading when the fever subsides. She reports pain and morning stiffness in her wrists, knees, and ankles during these two weeks. She denies any urinary or gastrointestinal symptoms. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She returned from Bali Indonesia seven weeks ago.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.9°C. There is a faint salmon-coloured macular rash on her trunk and upper arms. Her wrists and knees are tender with reduced range of motion but no obvious swelling, and power is bilaterally 5/5. Throat examination reveals mild pharyngitis.

Investigations reveal:

Haemoglobin: 110 g/L (115-165)
White cell count: 15.5 x 10⁹/L (4.0-11.0)
Neutrophils: 13.2 x 10⁹/L (2.0-7.5)
Platelets: 450 x 10⁹/L (150-400)
C-reactive protein: 120 mg/L (<5)
Ferritin: 3500 µg/L (15-200)
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Anti-nuclear antibodies: negative
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic triad of adult-onset Still's disease: daily spiking high-fevers at similar times which self-resolve, salmon-pink rash which fades with the normalisation of the fever, and arthralgia. The markedly elevated ferritin level (>1000 µg/L) is particularly characteristic of this condition, often more than five times the normal limit though this is non-specific. Patients may also present atypically with non-pruiritic papules, pruiritic plaques, pigmented plaques, flagellate morphology or dusky red macules so clinical diagnosis is difficult to base on the rash alone.

Adult-onset Still's disease is a rare immune-mediated inflammatory disorder that typically affects young adults between 16 - 35 years. Children present with similar symptoms which is know as systemic-onset juvenile idiopathic arthritis (SoJIA or previously juvenile Still's disease). The diagnosis of Adult-onset Still's disease is based on clinical criteria, as there is no definitive diagnostic test. The Yamaguchi criteria are most commonly used, requiring the presence of major criteria (fever, arthralgia, typical rash, leukocytosis) and minor criteria (sore throat, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, abnormal liver function tests, negative RF and ANA) after exclusion of other conditions.

Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS) typically presents 2-6 weeks after starting a new medication with fever, rash, and internal organ involvement. The rash is typically a morbilliform eruption that persists rather than being evanescent, and marked eosinophilia would be expected.

Reactive arthritis typically follows a gastrointestinal or genitourinary infection and presents with the classic triad of arthritis, conjunctivitis, and urethritis - the later two which is not present in this case. The rash in reactive arthritis is typically keratoderma blennorrhagicum, which appears quite different from the salmon-pink rash described.

Dengue fever can cause fever and rash, but typically presents with severe myalgia, headache, and retro-orbital pain. The rash typically appears 3-4 days after fever onset and is maculopapular, petechial or diffuse erythematous. Given the travel history to Bali, dengue should be considered but the pattern of presentation of 7 weeks since return (as incubation period is 3 - 14 days only) and laboratory findings are more consistent with Still's disease.

Dermatomyositis presents with a characteristic heliotrope rash and Gottron's papules, along with proximal muscle weakness. While it can cause fever, the rash is fixed rather than evanescent, and the pattern of muscle involvement would be different showing weakness instead of the arthralgia.
learningPoints: Adult-onset Still's disease classically presents with daily spiking fevers, salmon-pink rash, and arthralgia.
The characteristic rash in Still's disease is evanescent, salmon-pink, and most prominent during fever spikes.
Ferritin levels are typically markedly elevated (>1000 µg/L) in Adult-onset Still's disease.
references: Mehta B, Efthimiou P. Ferritin in adult-onset still's disease: just a useful innocent bystander? Int J Inflam. 2012;2012:298405.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3321299/
Ngan V, Oakley A.  Adult-onset Still disease. DermNet NZ. 2015.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/adult-onset-still-disease

Vora P et al. Atypical Adult-onset Still's disease with flagellate morphology in a patient with skin of color.  Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology Case Report. 2023: 37 (54-57).  Available at: https://www.jaadcasereports.org/article/S2352-5126(23)00145-5/fulltext
